informal: For any two real sequences $\left\{a_{n}\right\},\left\{b_{n}\right\}$, prove that $\limsup _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(a_{n}+b_{n}\right) \leq \limsup _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}+\limsup _{n \rightarrow \infty} b_{n},$ provided the sum on the right is not of the form $\infty-\infty$.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_3_5_corrected
    (a b : ℕ → EReal)
    (_hareal : ∀ n, a n ≠ ⊤ ∧ a n ≠ ⊥)
    (_hbreal : ∀ n, b n ≠ ⊤ ∧ b n ≠ ⊥)
    (_ha : limsup a ≠ ⊤)
    (hb : limsup b ≠ ⊥)
    (havoid : limsup a ≠ ⊥ ∨ limsup b ≠ ⊤) :
    limsup (λ n => a n + b n) ≤ limsup a + limsup b
reasoning: The informal claim's only side condition is that limsup a + limsup b is not of the form ∞−∞, i.e., the pair (limsup a, limsup b) is not (+∞,−∞) or (−∞,+∞). The Lean signature instead imposes the stronger hypotheses limsup a ≠ ⊤ and limsup b ≠ ⊥, plus a disjunction ruling out (−∞,+∞). This excludes legitimate cases such as limsup a = +∞ with limsup b = +∞ (sum = +∞, no ∞−∞ issue), or limsup a finite with limsup b = −∞, which the informal statement covers. So the Lean theorem is logically weaker: it is a special case implied by the informal claim but does not imply it.
